events.title: Juan Díaz de Solís Reaches Río de la Plata
events.detail: Spanish explorer Juan Díaz de Solís becomes the first European to reach the Río de la Plata estuary in February 1516, claiming the region for the Spanish Crown. Shortly after landing he is killed by indigenous Charrúa people, but his voyage opens South America's Atlantic coast to European exploration.

events.title: Pedro de Mendoza Founds Buenos Aires
events.detail: Pedro de Mendoza establishes the first European settlement at the port of Buenos Aires (Ciudad de la Santísima Trinidad y Puerto de Santa María del Buen Ayre) on February 2, 1536. Intense resistance from the Querandí people forces the settlers to abandon the site within five years.

sources.name: AGN — Archivo General de la Nación Argentina

events.title: Juan de Garay Permanently Refounds Buenos Aires
events.detail: Juan de Garay re-establishes Buenos Aires as a permanent settlement on June 11, 1580. The city grows into a contraband hub, bypassing Lima-centered Spanish trade restrictions. Its Atlantic position foreshadows its eventual dominance over the entire Río de la Plata region.

sources.name: Ciudad Autónoma de Buenos Aires — Official History

events.title: Jesuits Expelled — Río de la Plata Missions Collapse
events.detail: King Carlos III expels the Society of Jesus from all Spanish territories in 1767, dismantling the network of Jesuit Reductions (Misiones) that had protected and organized tens of thousands of Guaraní people in the northeast. This leaves indigenous communities unprotected and disrupts the region's agricultural economy.

sources.name: UNESCO World Heritage — Jesuit Missions of Argentina

events.title: Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata Established
events.detail: Spain creates the Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata on August 1, 1776, with Buenos Aires as its capital, separating the region from Lima's jurisdiction. This elevates Buenos Aires' administrative status and enables direct trade with Spain, rapidly transforming its commercial and demographic profile.

sources.name: AGN — Archivo General de la Nación Argentina

events.title: British Invasions of Buenos Aires Repelled
events.detail: British forces under Beresford seize Buenos Aires in June 1806 but are expelled by a local militia reconquista in August — without Spanish Crown help. A second invasion in 1807 is also repelled. The victories build profound local self-confidence and weaken loyalty to Spain, laying essential groundwork for independence.

sources.name: UK National Archives — River Plate Expedition 1806–07

events.title: May Revolution — First Independent Government
events.detail: On May 25, 1810, Buenos Aires' Cabildo replaces Viceroy Baltasar Hidalgo de Cisneros with a Primera Junta after Napoleon's invasion of Spain destabilizes colonial authority. This date — Día de la Patria — is celebrated as Argentina's national day, though independence is not formally declared until 1816.

events.title: Declaration of Independence at Tucumán
events.detail: The Congress of Tucumán formally declares independence from Spain on July 9, 1816, at the Casa Histórica in San Miguel de Tucumán. General José de San Martín's Army of the Andes is simultaneously preparing to cross into Chile to liberate Spanish South America from the Pacific side.

sources.name: Casa Histórica de la Independencia — Official Record

events.title: San Martín Crosses the Andes — Liberation of South America
events.detail: General José de San Martín leads the Army of the Andes (approximately 5,000 troops) across the Andes in January 1817, defeating Spanish royalist forces at the Battle of Chacabuco on February 12. This initiates the liberation of Chile and eventually Peru, cementing San Martín's status as the continent's greatest liberator.

sources.name: Argentine Ministry of Defense — Historical Division

events.title: Juan Manuel de Rosas — Federalist Dictatorship
events.detail: Federalist caudillo Juan Manuel de Rosas assumes near-absolute power in Buenos Aires Province in 1835 with the 'Sum of Public Power.' His 17-year regime suppresses liberal Unitarian opponents, forcing intellectuals such as Sarmiento, Alberdi, and Echeverría into exile. His rule defines the federalist-unitarian conflict that shapes Argentine political culture.

sources.name: Lynch, John — Argentine Caudillo: Juan Manuel de Rosas (2001)

events.title: Battle of Caseros — Fall of Rosas
events.detail: General Justo José de Urquiza defeats Rosas' forces at the Battle of Caseros on February 3, 1852, ending his 17-year dictatorship. Rosas flees to England where he dies in 1877. The victory opens the path for Argentina's constitutional organization and reunification.

events.title: Argentine Constitution Adopted
events.detail: Argentina adopts its federal constitution on May 1, 1853, heavily influenced by Juan Bautista Alberdi's 'Bases y puntos de partida.' The constitution establishes a federal republic, guarantees individual liberties, and declares 'gobernar es poblar' (to govern is to populate), opening the country to mass European immigration.

events.title: Conquest of the Desert — Indigenous Displacement
events.detail: General Julio Argentino Roca leads a military campaign (1878–1885) that defeats and displaces Mapuche, Tehuelche, and Ranquel indigenous peoples of Patagonia, seizing ~15 million hectares. At the time celebrated as national progress; today recognized by scholars and human rights groups as a campaign of ethnic cleansing and genocide.

sources.name: Human Rights Watch — Indigenous Peoples Argentina

events.title: Mass European Immigration Wave (1880s–1930)
events.detail: Over 6.5 million European immigrants — primarily Italian and Spanish, followed by Jewish, German, Syrian-Lebanese, and Eastern European arrivals — reshape Argentina between 1880 and 1930. Buenos Aires becomes one of the world's ten largest cities; immigrant labor drives the agro-export boom that makes Argentina among the world's wealthiest nations per capita.

sources.name: Devoto, Fernando — Historia de la Inmigración en la Argentina (2003)

events.title: Sáenz Peña Law — Universal Male Suffrage
events.detail: President Roque Sáenz Peña enacts Law 8,871 on February 13, 1912, establishing universal, secret, and compulsory male suffrage. This landmark reform ends decades of electoral fraud controlled by the landed oligarchy and enables Hipólito Yrigoyen's Radical Party to win Argentina's first genuinely free election in 1916.

sources.name: Romero, Luis Alberto — Breve Historia Contemporánea de la Argentina

events.title: Yrigoyen Elected — First Popular Government
events.detail: Hipólito Yrigoyen of the Unión Cívica Radical wins Argentina's first free presidential election on April 2, 1916, representing the first non-oligarchic government in Argentine history. His presidency (1916–1922) is marked by nationalist reform but also the bloody Semana Trágica labor suppression (1919) and Patagonia Rebelde massacres (1921–22).

events.title: Semana Trágica — Labor Massacre
events.detail: A general strike in Buenos Aires metalworking shops escalates into a week-long uprising (January 7–14, 1919). President Yrigoyen deploys the army; right-wing Liga Patriótica Argentina vigilantes attack Jewish and labor communities. Approximately 700 workers are killed and ~4,000 injured — one of the bloodiest episodes of labor repression in Argentine history.

events.title: First Military Coup — September 6, 1930
events.detail: General José Félix Uriburu overthrows President Hipólito Yrigoyen on September 6, 1930 — Argentina's first military coup. This ends 14 years of Radical Party government and inaugurates a pattern of military intervention in politics that recurs for 53 years. The coup is supported by conservative landowners and nationalist officers.

sources.name: Potash, Robert — The Army and Politics in Argentina (1969)

events.title: La Década Infame — Electoral Fraud Era
events.detail: The 'Infamous Decade' (1930–1943) is characterized by electoral fraud, political corruption, and conservative-military collusion. Pro-British economic policies during the Great Depression deepen social inequality. The Roca-Runciman Pact (1933) is denounced as a surrender of national sovereignty. Labor and leftist movements grow underground.

sources.name: Romero, Luis Alberto — Breve Historia Contemporánea de la Argentina

events.title: GOU Military Coup — Path to Perón
events.detail: The GOU (Grupo de Oficiales Unidos), a nationalist military lodge, overthrows the conservative Castillo government on June 4, 1943. Among the key plotters is Colonel Juan Domingo Perón, who exploits his position as Labor Secretary to forge an unprecedented alliance with organized labor that becomes the mass base of Peronism.

events.title: Perón Builds Power Through Labor
events.detail: As Secretary of Labor (1943–1945), Juan Domingo Perón allies with unions, forcing employers to grant wage increases, paid vacations, and improved conditions. He creates an enormous working-class constituency — los descamisados (the shirtless ones) — that will form Peronism's electoral core for generations. Conservative military officers, alarmed by his power, arrest him in October 1945.

sources.name: James, Daniel — Resistance and Integration: Peronism (1988)

events.title: October 17, 1945 — Día de la Lealtad
events.detail: After Perón's arrest on October 9, 1945, hundreds of thousands of workers march on Buenos Aires from industrial suburbs demanding his release. This 'Día de la Lealtad' (Day of Loyalty) becomes the founding myth of Peronism. Perón is freed, addresses the crowd from the Casa Rosada balcony, and immediately announces his presidential candidacy.

sources.name: James, Daniel — Resistance and Integration: Peronism

events.title: Perón Elected President — Transforming Argentina
events.detail: Juan Domingo Perón wins the presidential election on February 24, 1946, with 54% of the vote, defeating the Democratic Union backed by the US Embassy. His program of economic nationalism, state-led industrialization (ISI), labor rights expansion, and social welfare (free healthcare, education, housing) fundamentally transforms Argentina's political economy.

events.title: Women's Suffrage — Evita Champions the Vote
events.detail: Argentina grants women the right to vote on September 23, 1947 (Law 13,010), largely through the championing of Eva Perón. Women vote for the first time in November 1951; they overwhelmingly support Perón, who wins 63% of the vote in that election.

events.title: Eva Perón Dies — National Mourning
events.detail: María Eva Duarte de Perón ('Evita'), who championed women's suffrage, social welfare programs, and working-class rights, dies of cervical cancer on July 26, 1952, aged 33. Her death triggers mass national mourning; millions file past her body over 13 days. She becomes an enduring icon of Peronism, Argentine identity, and global left-wing movements.

events.title: Revolución Libertadora — Perón Overthrown
events.detail: On September 16, 1955, a military coup led by General Eduardo Lonardi and Admiral Isaac Rojas overthrows Perón, who flees to Paraguay (later Spain). The coup follows months of conflict including the naval bombing of Plaza de Mayo on June 16, 1955, killing ~300 civilians. Peronism is banned for 18 years; Perón's name cannot be printed in newspapers.

sources.name: Luna, Félix — De Perón a Lanusse (1972)

events.title: Frondizi and Desarrollismo
events.detail: Arturo Frondizi wins the presidency in 1958 with secret Peronist support. His 'desarrollismo' (developmentalism) promotes oil self-sufficiency and heavy industrial investment. Military pressure mounts over his pragmatic leftward gestures. He is overthrown in a coup in March 1962, only to be replaced by another elected government in 1963 that is also soon overthrown.

sources.name: Wynia, Gary — Argentina: Illusions and Realities (1992)
Romero, Luis Alberto — Breve Historia Contemporánea de la Argentina

events.title: El Cordobazo — Urban Uprising Shakes Dictatorship
events.detail: On May 29–30, 1969, workers and students in Córdoba mount a massive uprising against General Juan Carlos Onganía's military dictatorship. The 'Cordobazo' paralyzes the city, kills ~30 people, and marks a turning point that galvanizes guerrilla organizations (ERP, Montoneros) and ultimately destabilizes the entire military regime, forcing Onganía's resignation in 1970.

sources.name: Brennan, James — The Labor Wars in Córdoba (1994)

events.title: Perón Returns — Ezeiza Massacre
events.detail: After 18 years in exile, Juan Perón returns to Argentina on June 20, 1973. Right-wing Peronists fire on leftist Montonero youth welcoming him at Ezeiza airport, killing ~13 and injuring ~365 in the 'Ezeiza Massacre.' Perón wins re-election in September 1973 but dies in office on July 1, 1974, leaving power to wife Isabel and the sinister López Rega.

events.title: Military Coup — Start of Dirty War
events.detail: General Jorge Rafael Videla leads a military coup on March 24, 1976, overthrowing President Isabel Perón. The 'Process of National Reorganization' (El Proceso) begins systematic state terrorism: ~350 secret detention centers open nationwide, where opponents are tortured and executed. 'Death flights' dump living prisoners into the Río de la Plata. Between 9,000 and 30,000 people 'disappear.'

events.title: ESMA — Argentina's Most Notorious Detention Center
events.detail: The Escuela Superior de Mecánica de la Armada (ESMA) in Buenos Aires operates as the regime's most notorious clandestine detention center (1976–1983). Approximately 5,000 prisoners pass through; fewer than 200 survive. Navy officers conduct 'death flights' (vuelos de la muerte), pushing sedated live prisoners from aircraft into the sea. Today the site is a Memory and Human Rights Museum.

events.title: Malvinas/Falklands War (April–June 1982)
events.detail: On April 2, 1982, the Argentine military junta under General Leopoldo Galtieri invades the Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas), controlled by Britain since 1833. A British naval task force retakes the islands by June 14, 1982. Argentina suffers 649 dead (323 from the sinking of ARA General Belgrano); Britain loses 255. The defeat destroys the junta's credibility and accelerates the return to democracy.

sources.name: Argentine Ministry of Defense — Official Casualty Count
Freedman, Lawrence — Official History of the Falklands Campaign (2005)

events.title: Alfonsín Elected — Return to Democracy
events.detail: Raúl Alfonsín of the Radical Party (UCR) defeats Peronist candidate Ítalo Luder on October 30, 1983, with 51.7% of the vote — the first time the UCR defeats Peronism. He is inaugurated on December 10, 1983, ending 7 years of brutal military rule. His election is celebrated worldwide as Argentina's democratic rebirth.

events.title: Trial of the Juntas — Landmark Transitional Justice
events.detail: In 1985, Argentina conducts the world's first successful criminal prosecution of an entire military government for human rights crimes. The Federal Court convicts nine junta leaders; General Videla and Admiral Massera receive life sentences. The trial, based on CONADEP's Nunca Más report, sets a global precedent for transitional justice later followed by Chile, Spain, and other nations.

sources.name: Cámara Federal de Buenos Aires — Verdict, December 1985

events.title: Full-Stop and Obedience Laws — Limits on Justice
events.detail: Under military pressure following the Carapintada rebellions, President Alfonsín signs the Ley de Punto Final (1986) and Ley de Obediencia Debida (1987), setting a statute of limitations on new prosecutions and exempting lower-ranking officers. These laws effectively halt most ongoing cases and are condemned by human rights groups; they are later annulled by Congress in 2003.

events.title: Hyperinflation Crisis — Alfonsín Resigns Early
events.detail: Argentina's economy implodes with annual inflation reaching 3,079% in 1989, triggering food riots and looting. President Alfonsín transfers power to Peronist winner Carlos Menem six months early on July 8, 1989 — only the second democratic civilian-to-civilian transfer in Argentine history, underscoring the fragility of the restored democracy.

events.title: Convertibility Plan — 1 Peso = 1 Dollar
events.detail: Economy Minister Domingo Cavallo implements the Convertibility Plan on April 1, 1991, pegging the peso to the US dollar at 1:1 and legally prohibiting the central bank from printing money beyond its dollar reserves. This immediately eliminates hyperinflation and fuels a boom, but steadily overvalues Argentine exports and creates a structural trade imbalance.

events.title: AMIA Bombing — 85 Killed in Worst Terror Attack
events.detail: On July 18, 1994, a car bomb destroys the AMIA (Asociación Mutual Israelita Argentina) building in Buenos Aires, killing 85 people and injuring 300+. It remains the deadliest terrorist attack in Argentine history and the worst antisemitic attack in the Western Hemisphere since WWII. Argentina officially accuses Iran and Hezbollah; perpetrators have never been prosecuted in Argentina.

sources.name: Argentine Federal Justice — AMIA Case Records

events.title: Mass Privatizations Under Menem
events.detail: President Menem privatizes virtually all state enterprises between 1989–1994: YPF (oil), ENTEL (telephone), Aerolíneas Argentinas, national railroads, water utilities, and electricity. Proceeds partially fund budget deficits but dismantle the state's role in strategic sectors and result in mass unemployment in provincial industrial towns.

events.title: 2001 Economic Collapse — Corralito and Five Presidents
events.detail: On December 1, 2001, Economy Minister Cavallo freezes bank accounts ('corralito') to stop capital flight. Protests erupt; on December 19–20, police kill 39 demonstrators ('Argentinazo'). President de la Rúa flees the Casa Rosada by helicopter on December 20. Argentina goes through 5 presidents in 12 days and defaults on $100 billion in sovereign debt — the largest default in history at that time.

events.title: Pesification — End of the Dollar Peg
events.detail: President Eduardo Duhalde ends the convertibility peg in January 2002, converting dollar deposits to pesos at asymmetric rates, causing massive wealth destruction for savers. GDP contracts 10.9% in 2002; unemployment reaches 21.5%; poverty surges to 57%. The peso collapses from 1:1 to nearly 4:1 against the dollar by year's end.

events.title: Néstor Kirchner Elected — Post-Crisis Recovery
events.detail: Néstor Kirchner of the Peronist Front for Victory (FPV) becomes president in May 2003 after Carlos Menem withdraws from the runoff. Kirchner leads Argentina's remarkable recovery (averaging ~8% GDP growth, 2003–2008), renegotiates the 2001 default at a ~70% haircut, and reopens human rights trials by annulling the Punto Final and Obediencia Debida laws.

events.title: Human Rights Trials Reopened
events.detail: Congress annuls the Ley de Punto Final and Ley de Obediencia Debida in August 2003; the Supreme Court declares them unconstitutional in 2005. Hundreds of new Dirty War cases are prosecuted. By 2024, over 1,000 perpetrators have been convicted — the most comprehensive accountability process for state terrorism in the world.

sources.name: Ministerio de Justicia — Programa Memoria, Verdad, Justicia

events.title: Cristina Fernández de Kirchner Elected
events.detail: Cristina Fernández de Kirchner wins the presidential election on October 28, 2007, with 45.3% in the first round, becoming Argentina's first elected female president. Her two terms (2007–2015) maintain statist economic policies, renationalize YPF (2012), and expand social programs — but are also marked by inflation denial, INDEC statistics manipulation, and the Nisman affair.

events.title: YPF Renationalized — Energy Sovereignty
events.detail: On April 16, 2012, President CFK expropriates 51% of YPF from Spain's Repsol. The move is framed as reclaiming energy sovereignty after Argentina shifted from net oil exporter to importer. Spain threatens trade retaliation; a $5B compensation deal is eventually reached in 2014. YPF then pioneers exploration of the Vaca Muerta shale formation.

events.title: Mauricio Macri Elected — Center-Right Turn
events.detail: Mauricio Macri of the Cambiemos coalition wins a presidential runoff on November 22, 2015, defeating Kirchnerist Daniel Scioli 51.3% to 48.7% — Argentina's first competitive runoff. Macri lifts foreign currency controls ('cepo'), settles with holdout creditors, and seeks IMF support. A 2018 currency crisis undoes his economic program; he loses re-election in 2019.

events.title: Argentina Secures Record $57B IMF Loan
events.detail: Amid catastrophic capital flight and a 50% currency collapse, President Macri agrees to the largest IMF loan in history — initially $50B expanded to $57B — in June 2018. Strict austerity conditions trigger mass protests and recession. The peso continues falling; Macri suffers a shock defeat in August 2019 primaries (PASO) and loses the general election to Kirchnerist Alberto Fernández.

events.title: Alberto Fernández Elected — Kirchnerism Returns
events.detail: Alberto Fernández, with Cristina Kirchner as vice presidential running mate, wins the election on October 27, 2019, with 48.1% vs Macri's 40.4%. His presidency (2019–2023) navigates COVID-19 lockdowns, a 2020 sovereign debt restructuring, spiraling inflation, and CFK's corruption conviction. Fernández does not run for re-election.

events.title: Javier Milei Elected — Libertarian Shock Therapy
events.detail: Javier Milei, a libertarian outsider economist brandishing a chainsaw at rallies, wins the presidential election on November 19, 2023, with 55.7% vs Kirchnerist Sergio Massa's 44.3%. Milei immediately devalues the peso 54%, slashes public spending, closes ministries, and abolishes price controls. He achieves Argentina's first primary fiscal surplus since 2011 by mid-2024.

events.title: Milei's New IMF Program and Capital Controls Lifted
events.detail: In April 2025, Argentina and the IMF agree to a new $20B Extended Fund Facility program — Milei's first major international financial deal. Simultaneously, Milei lifts the years-long currency exchange controls ('cepo cambiario') that had distorted Argentina's exchange rate. The peso unifies, and Argentina re-enters global capital markets.
